summary: I was born in Lima, Peru, but I moved away when I was only four years old, so I don't remember much about my early life there. Despite that, Lima is important to me because it's where my family's cultural roots come from. Even after moving, my family carried the traditions and culture of Lima with us to the United States.

Peru is my homeland, and although I left when I was very young, my family always kept our Peruvian culture alive in the United States. My parents continued to cook Peruvian food, listen to Peruvian music, and decorate our home with Peruvian textiles and artwork. Living in America, we balanced our new life with the traditions and identity we inherited from Peru.

When my family first arrived in the United States, we settled in Salt Lake City, Utah. It was a big change for us, and we had to learn English and adapt to a new culture while trying to keep our Peruvian identity. Salt Lake City became the place where we started our new life and worked hard to build a better future.

Our first home in Utah was near Cottonwood Heights, where we began adjusting to life in America. During those early years, my parents were very busy and stressed with their new jobs and learning English. Cottonwood Heights was where we first experienced the challenges and changes of immigration.

After some time, we moved to a nicer neighborhood called Suncrest, Utah. This move marked a positive turn for my family, as my parents were able to buy designer clothes, cars, and even develop our dream house. Suncrest represented the success and comfort we achieved after years of hard work and adaptation.

Spanish has always been a vital part of our family's identity, even after moving to the United States. We spoke Spanish at home, which helped us maintain our connection to Peruvian culture. Despite learning English and adapting to American life, Spanish remained the language that kept our heritage alive.
